x.name: Peter Yang
x.bio: Practical AI tutorials and interviews for busy people | Join 140K+ readers at https://t.co/XYKTmGVH14 | Product at Roblox

tweets.text:
If you want to know what the next generation expects from AI just use Codex with a 7 year old.

"What do you mean it can't build a pet dragon raising game instantly? I'm bored" https://t.co/M9wWoIGUtA
Are there good human-curated skill repos for design, coding, product, etc that's not "here's 100 skills you separate the slop yourself"
I often have to get Codex to fix my OpenClaw set up 🥲

The entire West Coast of the United States from Santa Cruz on up. SF, Marin, Seattle, Portland…

If only we built housing so that people could actually live there https://t.co/k4eBM6IgcU

The problem with being on the very bleeding edge of adoption is you’re going to constantly be changing how you do things as the industry learns. 

The sweet spot is just a couple steps behind. Whatever is relatively new, but survives all of the churn.

This is another way of saying the same thing, believe it or not 

https://t.co/mhISOTPiX4

x.name: Amanda Askell
x.bio:
Philosopher & ethicist trying to make AI be good @AnthropicAI.
Personal account. All opinions come from my training data.

tweets.text: What I'm learning from flight simulators is that it would be a bit boring to be an amateur cessna pilot but a lot of fun to be an amateur fighter jet pilot.

2000s: every company is an internet company

2010s: every company is a software company

2020s: every company is an AI company

2025+: every company is a cybersecurity company

x.name: Aaron Levie
x.bio: ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI

tweets.text:
Great read. AI lets you get tremendous leverage that wasn’t available before in almost any domain. 

That means we’re at a unique moment in history where anyone with a high level of ambition and core skills in any area can overcome a lot of historical experience requirements for their role.

This can apply to anyone who’s junior or senior, but it’s pretty sweet that you can do far more than you could have accomplished as a newer employee than even a couple years ago. The people that take advantage of this will get ahead massively. 

And the companies that find this talent within or outside should put them in key positions to get as much out of them as possible. These people will seem strange and from the future, but they will help you figure out where things are going. Everyone company should be doing whatever they can to find them.

x.name: Zara Zhang

tweets.text:
When I sit down at my computer and start typing, that's not the beginning of writing. It's the LAST step. 80% of the writing is already done. It just happened in my head.

- Writing ≠ generating text
- Building product ≠ writing PRDs
- Designing ≠ making mockups
- Engineering ≠ writing code

x.name: Nikunj Kothari
x.bio: partner @fpvventures - investing in seed/A. previous: early hire @meter, @opendoor, @atlassian & others. love @shimoleejhaveri + 👦👧

tweets.text: Every time I pass by my neighbors side door I get this visceral feeling this is a portal to another universe.. https://t.co/wmLKvqlRnw

Brevity and clarity is what’s missing in most writing..

I’ll go weeks without writing something publicly because I feel like I’m putting the onus of my muddy thoughts on the reader. 

The best writing is able to communicate cleanly the crux of the matter and share a thread that’s simmering but not explored fully. 

Writing is still easier since you can edit and reframe anytime. I’m in awe of folks who are able to speak clearly and on the spot on any topic. 

One day I hope to get there 🤞

podcasts.name: No Priors
podcasts.title: Scaling Global Organizations in the Age of AI with ServiceNow CEO Bill McDermott

digest:
AI Builders Digest — April 27, 2026

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
X / TWITTER
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Sam Altman — OpenAI CEO
Three rapid-fire posts: "We still get looksmaxxed on frontend a little but we IQmog hard now" + "and we will fix the former" — classic Sam understated brag after a big model week. Also posted a video with "how can they write code so fast?!" — likely referring to Codex's output speed.
https://x.com/sama/status/2048165186482389253

Sam Altman — OpenAI CEO
三连快发：「前端上我们还是会被碾一下，但智商上我们硬碾」+「而且我们会修好前者」—— Sam 式低调炫耀，大模型周后的标配。还发了一个视频感叹「他们怎么能写代码这么快？！」——大概率在说 Codex 的输出速度。
https://x.com/sama/status/2048165186482389253

---

Garry Tan — President & CEO @YCombinator
Three shipping posts: (1) GBrain v0.22 released — better search/retrieval, new eval system separated into its own repo. (2) GStack now has `/open-gstack-browser` skill, letting you control a browser side-by-side with Claude Code. (3) To install: open Claude Code and type "Install GStack".
https://x.com/garrytan/status/2048290087318773807

Garry Tan — YCombinator CEO
三条发布更新：(1) GBrain v0.22 上线——搜索/检索优化，新增独立 eval 系统。(2) GStack 新增 `/open-gstack-browser` 技能，可在 Claude Code 旁边直接操控浏览器。(3) 安装方式：打开 Claude Code，输入「Install GStack」。
https://x.com/garrytan/status/2048290087318773807

---

Amjad Masad — CEO @Replit
A crisp historical parallel: "2000s: every company is an internet company. 2010s: every company is a software company. 2020s: every company is an AI company." The pattern is clear — and the implication is that resistance is futile.
https://x.com/amasad/status/2048265772775162078

Amjad Masad — Replit CEO
一个清晰的历史规律：「00年代：每家公司都是互联网公司。10年代：每家公司都是软件公司。20年代：每家公司都是 AI 公司。」趋势已定——抵抗毫无意义。
https://x.com/amasad/status/2048265772775162078

---

Peter Steinberger — Builder & AI Tinkerer
Two tools shipped: (1) Summarize 0.14.0 — adds GPT-5.5 Fast mode via `--fast` flag, Reddit thread extraction in the browser extension, local PDF support. (2) CodexBar 0.23 — adds Mistral support, Claude usage tracking, GPT-5.5 pricing display. He's building a whole toolkit around AI dev tools.
https://x.com/steipete/status/2048275589224628677

Peter Steinberger — 构建者 & AI 探索者
两个工具更新：(1) Summarize 0.14.0——通过 `--fast` 参数支持 GPT-5.5 Fast 模式，浏览器扩展新增 Reddit 帖子提取，支持本地 PDF。(2) CodexBar 0.23——新增 Mistral 支持、Claude 用量追踪、GPT-5.5 定价展示。他正在围绕 AI 开发工具构建一整套工具链。
https://x.com/steipete/status/2048275589224628677

---

Aaron Levie — CEO @Box
One sentence summary of the current AI moment: "AI lets you get tremendous leverage that wasn't available before in almost any domain. That means we're at a critical inflection point." The leverage argument — that AI amplifies whatever you already do well — is becoming the central framing for enterprise AI adoption.
https://x.com/levie/status/2048223146063609965

Aaron Levie — Box CEO
一句话概括当下 AI 时刻：「AI 让你在几乎任何领域都能获得前所未有的巨大杠杆。这意味着我们正处于关键拐点。」杠杆论——AI 放大你本已擅长的事情——正在成为企业级 AI 采用的核心叙事框架。
https://x.com/levie/status/2048223146063609965

---

Peter Yang — AI Educator (140K+ readers)
Two sharp observations: (1) "If you want to know what the next generation expects from AI, use Codex with a 7-year-old. 'What do you mean it can't...'" — kids have zero patience for AI limitations. (2) Asking if there are good human-curated skill repos for design/coding/product — flagging a gap in the current AI tooling landscape.
https://x.com/petergyang/status/2048220045726322934

Peter Yang — AI 教育者（14万+读者）
两个犀利观察：(1)「想知道下一代对 AI 的期待？让一个 7 岁孩子用 Codex 试试。'你说它不能……是什么意思？'」——孩子们对 AI 的局限零容忍。(2) 在问有没有好的人工精选技能库（设计/编程/产品方向）——点出了当前 AI 工具生态的一个空白。
https://x.com/petergyang/status/2048220045726322934

---

Nan Yu — Head of Product @Linear
The bleeding edge problem: "Being on the very bleeding edge of adoption means you're going to constantly be changing how you do things." Not a complaint — an observation about the cost of being early. Also: a thread on SF housing that connects urban density to tech productivity.
https://x.com/thenanyu/status/2048172709075402787

Nan Yu — Linear 产品负责人
最前沿的代价：「处于采用最前沿意味着你要不断改变做事方式。」不是抱怨——是对早期入场成本的清醒观察。还发了一个关于旧金山住房的帖子，将城市密度与科技生产力联系起来。
https://x.com/thenanyu/status/2048172709075402787

---

Swyx — AI Engineer & Writer
Three posts: (1) Teasing something cooking with @hhua_ — "keep calendar open after ICML in Seoul." (2) Sharing an essay on "code is not cheap" — the counter-narrative to "AI makes coding free." (3) PM vs engineer perspective meme on a shipping scenario.
https://x.com/swyx/status/2048126046487322835

Swyx — AI 工程师与作家
三条更新：(1) 预告与 @hhua_ 正在合作一个项目——「ICML 首尔站之后记得留好日历」。(2) 转发了一篇「代码并不便宜」的文章——对「AI 让编程免费」叙事的反驳。(3) 关于发布场景中 PM 视角 vs 工程师视角的梗图。
https://x.com/swyx/status/2048126046487322835

---

Zara Zhang — Builder
On writing process: "When I sit down at my computer and start typing, that's not the beginning of writing. It's the LAST step. 80% of the writing happens before I open the document." A reminder that thinking and writing are not the same thing — and that the bottleneck is rarely the typing.
https://x.com/zarazhangrui/status/2048101948826751429

Zara Zhang — 构建者
关于写作过程：「当我坐下来开始打字，那不是写作的开始。那是最后一步。80% 的写作发生在我打开文档之前。」提醒我们思考和写作不是一回事——瓶颈很少在于打字本身。
https://x.com/zarazhangrui/status/2048101948826751429

---

Amanda Askell — Philosopher & AI Ethicist @Anthropic
A delightfully unexpected take: what she's learning from flight simulators is that being an amateur Cessna pilot would be boring, but a lot of fun to be something more. The kind of post that makes you wonder what Anthropic's safety researchers do in their spare time.
https://x.com/AmandaAskell/status/2048294481955631163

Amanda Askell — Anthropic 哲学家 & AI 伦理学家
出人意料的一条：她从飞行模拟器学到的是，做一个业余塞斯纳飞行员会很无聊，但更高阶的飞行会很有趣。这种帖子会让你好奇 Anthropic 的安全研究员们业余时间都在干什么。
https://x.com/AmandaAskell/status/2048294481955631163

---

Dan Shipper — CEO @Every
A philosophy of knowledge in one sentence: "What we observe is never the model itself, only the model exposed to our method of questioning." Epistemology meets AI — how you ask shapes what you see.
https://x.com/danshipper/status/2048026505461080076

Dan Shipper — Every CEO
一句话知识论：「我们观察到的从来不是模型本身，只是模型在我们的提问方式下的表现。」认识论遇上 AI——你怎么问，决定了你看到什么。
https://x.com/danshipper/status/2048026505461080076

---

Nikunj Kothari — Partner @FPV Ventures
On writing: "Brevity and clarity is what's missing in most writing. I'll go weeks without writing something publicly because I feel like I don't have anything concise enough to say." A VC who actually practices what he preaches about signal-to-noise ratio.
https://x.com/nikunj/status/2048056524845482131

Nikunj Kothari — FPV Ventures 合伙人
关于写作：「大多数写作缺少的是简洁和清晰。我会好几周不公开发任何东西，因为我觉得自己还没有足够精炼的内容可说。」一位真正践行信噪比原则的 VC。
https://x.com/nikunj/status/2048056524845482131

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PODCAST
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

No Priors — ServiceNow CEO Bill McDermott: Scaling Global Organizations in the Age of AI
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tNNFJa5pUEg

The Takeaway: Enterprise software doesn't die — it evolves. And the companies that built deep process knowledge over decades are better positioned for the AI era than the startups trying to replace them.

Bill McDermott, CEO of ServiceNow (market cap ~$170B), is one of the most successful enterprise software operators alive. The episode opens with a striking argument: replacing an enterprise platform isn't just a software cost. Add the human capital cost of migration, plus the GPU/token costs of the AI alternative, and for a "simple" application, it's 10x more expensive to rebuild with an LLM than to stay on the existing platform.

His core philosophy, forged running a deli at age 16: "The customer and the customer alone determines whether you win or lose." This customer-first obsession is how he scaled SAP from $7B to $30B in revenue before moving to ServiceNow. The enterprise lesson that translates to AI: people never forgive software for making a mistake. That's the bar AI products need to clear — not just "good enough," but trustworthy at enterprise scale.

On leadership in the AI era: the question isn't whether to adopt AI, but how fast. McDermott argues that global organizations that move too slowly will find themselves competitively disadvantaged within 18-24 months. The window for "wait and see" has closed.

One underappreciated point: ServiceNow's advantage isn't its technology — it's the institutional knowledge baked into its workflows. That's not something an LLM can replicate from scratch. The moat is process depth, not code.

---

No Priors — ServiceNow CEO Bill McDermott：AI 时代的全球组织规模化
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tNNFJa5pUEg

要点：企业软件不会消亡——它在进化。几十年积累深厚流程知识的公司，在 AI 时代的位置比那些试图取代它们的初创企业更稳固。

ServiceNow CEO Bill McDermott（市值约 1700 亿美元）是当今最成功的企业软件运营者之一。本集开篇抛出一个犀利论点：替换一个企业平台不只是软件成本。加上迁移的人力成本，再加上 AI 替代方案的 GPU/token 成本，对于一个"简单"应用，用 LLM 重建比留在现有平台贵 10 倍。

他的核心哲学，来自 16 岁经营熟食店的经历：「最终，客户也只有客户决定你是赢是输。」正是这种以客户为中心的执念，让他把 SAP 从 70 亿美元做到 300 亿美元，然后又来到 ServiceNow。可以直接映射到 AI 的企业教训：人们永远不会原谅软件犯错。这就是 AI 产品需要跨越的门槛——不是「够用就行」，而是在企业规模下值得信赖。

关于 AI 时代的领导力：问题不是要不要采用 AI，而是有多快。McDermott 认为，行动太慢的全球组织会在 18-24 个月内发现自己竞争力落后。「观望」的窗口已经关闭。

一个被低估的观点：ServiceNow 的优势不在于技术——在于其工作流中积累的机构知识。这不是 LLM 能从头复制的东西。护城河是流程深度，不是代码。

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
